title: Avoid using tx.origin for authentication
rationale: tx.origin can be abused through phishing-style call chains and should not be used for authorization.
recommendation: Use msg.sender plus explicit role checks or AccessControl.

title: Sensitive setter appears to be missing access control
rationale: Admin configuration functions should be restricted to privileged roles.
recommendation: Protect privileged configuration with onlyOwner, AccessControl, or explicit admin checks.

title: Legacy constructor pattern may be callable after deployment
rationale: In old Solidity versions, constructor logic depended on the function name matching the contract name exactly. A mismatched initializer can remain publicly callable and let attackers seize ownership or reset critical state.
recommendation: Replace name-based constructor logic with the constructor keyword or guard the initializer so it can only run once.

title: Initializer appears callable without one-time guard
rationale: Public initializer flows that assign privileged roles or flip initialization state can be abused if they are callable more than once or lack an authorization check.
recommendation: Protect initialization with initializer/reinitializer modifiers or an explicit initialized guard and restrict who may trigger setup.

title: Ownership transfer function appears unprotected
rationale: Functions that directly change owner or admin addresses are critical control surfaces and should never be callable by arbitrary users.
recommendation: Restrict owner/admin transfer functions with explicit access control and consider two-step ownership transfers.

title: Pause control appears callable without access control
rationale: Pause and unpause flows are privileged control paths and should be restricted to trusted operators.
recommendation: Protect pause functions with onlyOwner, AccessControl, or an equivalent privileged-role check.

title: Upgrade or implementation setter appears unprotected
rationale: Upgrade entry points are among the highest-risk functions in a proxy system because arbitrary callers may swap the implementation and fully compromise the protocol.
recommendation: Restrict upgrade flows with explicit authorization and add upgrade event logging plus implementation validation.

title: External call is executed before state reset
rationale: Calling an external address before clearing state opens a classical reentrancy window.
recommendation: Apply checks-effects-interactions and clear state before the external call, or use ReentrancyGuard.

title: Withdrawal-like flow performs external payment without visible reentrancy guard
rationale: Withdraw, claim and unstake flows are common reentrancy targets when value is sent out before the function completes and there is no clear guard.
recommendation: Use checks-effects-interactions, nonReentrant, and state accounting that is finalized before any external payment is attempted.

title: Raw low-level call pattern detected (manual review)
rationale: Mature protocols often use low-level call intentionally. This heuristic is useful for exploratory review but can over-report without flow-sensitive checks.
recommendation: Treat this as a manual-review hint. Confirm return-value checks, gas/griefing assumptions, and reentrancy safety on each call site.

title: Ether send appears unchecked
rationale: send() returns a boolean instead of reverting. Ignoring the return value can silently skip payouts or break accounting assumptions.
recommendation: Check the send() result explicitly or switch to a safer payment pattern with require-based error handling.

title: delegatecall detected
rationale: delegatecall executes external code in the current storage context and can break invariants or open upgrade and authorization risks.
recommendation: Review delegatecall targets, storage layout assumptions, and access control around every execution path.

title: selfdestruct or suicide opcode usage detected
rationale: Contract destruction paths can break protocol assumptions, destroy state, and redirect remaining ETH unexpectedly.
recommendation: Avoid selfdestruct in production systems unless there is a rigorously reviewed and access-controlled shutdown design.

title: Block timestamp used as a randomness source
rationale: Block properties are not secure randomness sources for adversarial settings such as lotteries or NFT mint randomness.
recommendation: Use a verifiable randomness solution such as Chainlink VRF or an off-chain commit-reveal design.

title: blockhash-based randomness detected
rationale: blockhash, block.number and similar chain inputs are adversarially influenceable and should not be treated as secure randomness.
recommendation: Replace block-derived randomness with VRF or a carefully designed commit-reveal scheme.

title: Potential unbounded loop over user-controlled state
rationale: Loops over dynamic arrays or participant sets can become uncallable as the dataset grows and may create denial-of-service conditions.
recommendation: Use pull-based settlement, bounded batching, or off-chain indexing instead of unbounded on-chain iteration.

title: Looped payout or push-payment pattern detected
rationale: Sending funds inside loops can make settlement fail or become too expensive as the recipient set grows.
recommendation: Move to pull payments or batched withdrawals with bounded work per transaction.

title: ERC20 transfer return value may be ignored
rationale: Many ERC20 implementations signal failure via a false return value instead of reverting. Ignoring the result can silently break accounting or payout logic.
recommendation: Use SafeERC20 or explicitly require successful transfer/transferFrom/approve return values.

title: Signature flow may be missing replay protection
rationale: Generic ecrecover or ECDSA verification flows need nonce, deadline and domain separation to avoid replay across users, chains or time windows.
recommendation: Bind signatures to signer, chainId, nonce, deadline and the exact action or resource being authorized.

title: Oracle usage lacks visible staleness checks
rationale: Price-sensitive lending and collateral flows should validate oracle freshness, answeredInRound and heartbeat assumptions.
recommendation: Check updatedAt, answeredInRound, heartbeat windows and fallback behavior before trusting oracle responses.

title: Legacy compiler with unchecked arithmetic detected
rationale: Solidity versions before 0.8 do not include built-in overflow checks. Plain arithmetic in legacy code deserves manual review unless SafeMath or equivalent protections are used consistently.
recommendation: Use Solidity 0.8+ checked arithmetic or wrap every critical calculation with SafeMath-style guards.

title: Spot DEX price appears to be used without TWAP protection
rationale: Using a raw AMM spot price from pair reserves or slot0 in price-sensitive logic is a common DeFi failure mode because it can be manipulated within a single block or flash-loan transaction.
recommendation: Use a TWAP or hardened oracle layer such as Uniswap OracleLibrary/observe-based windows, Chainlink, or a bounded multi-source oracle.

title: Oracle integration lacks visible sanity bounds or circuit breaker
rationale: Even with a fresh oracle, protocols need safety rails such as bounds checks, sequencer checks, or circuit breakers to handle extreme values and oracle outages safely.
recommendation: Add sanity bounds, sequencer checks for L2 feeds, fallback behavior, and a circuit-breaker path for abnormal oracle values.

title: Oracle value usage lacks visible decimal normalization
rationale: Mixing oracle answers, token decimals and protocol precision without explicit normalization is a common source of collateral and pricing bugs in lending and vault systems.
recommendation: Normalize every oracle answer into a clearly documented fixed-point format and account for feed and token decimals explicitly.

title: L2 oracle integration lacks visible sequencer uptime checks
rationale: On optimistic rollups and similar L2s, price-sensitive flows should account for sequencer downtime and grace periods. Otherwise stale or unsafe prices may be trusted immediately after sequencer recovery.
recommendation: Integrate a sequencer uptime feed and enforce a grace period before accepting oracle prices after sequencer downtime.

title: Flash-loan callback lacks visible lender verification
rationale: Flash-loan callback entry points must validate the caller. If arbitrary users can invoke the callback, they may bypass intended flow assumptions or trigger sensitive code paths directly.
recommendation: Restrict flash-loan callback functions so only the trusted lending pool or flash-loan provider may call them.

title: Flash-loan callback lacks visible initiator check
rationale: Many flash-loan integrations also need to validate that the initiator is the current contract. Otherwise borrowed execution may be re-routed through an unexpected initiator context.
recommendation: Check that the initiator is address(this) or another explicitly trusted initiator before continuing execution.

title: Swap flow lacks visible slippage protection
rationale: AMM swaps without amountOutMin or an equivalent bound are exposed to sandwich attacks, stale routing assumptions and unexpected execution prices.
recommendation: Require amountOutMin or an equivalent execution-price bound for every user-facing swap path.

title: Swap flow lacks visible deadline or expiry protection
rationale: Swap requests without deadlines are easier to replay or execute after market conditions have materially changed.
recommendation: Attach an explicit deadline or signed expiry to every user-directed swap and check it before execution.

title: Swap flow appears to set minimum output to zero
rationale: Passing a zero minimum output disables slippage protection in practice and leaves the swap vulnerable to poor execution and sandwich attacks.
recommendation: Compute and enforce a non-zero minimum output based on user-configured slippage tolerance or a trusted quote source.

title: Borrow or redemption flow lacks visible solvency checks
rationale: Borrow, withdraw and redeem flows in lending systems should validate post-action solvency or health-factor constraints. Missing checks can allow undercollateralized exits.
recommendation: Enforce health-factor, collateral-ratio or solvency checks after every state-changing borrowing or collateral withdrawal path.

title: Liquidation flow lacks visible close-factor or incentive bounds
rationale: Liquidation logic usually needs explicit bounds such as close factor, liquidation bonus caps and repayment limits. Missing parameters can cause overly aggressive or inconsistent liquidations.
recommendation: Define close-factor and liquidation incentive bounds, and validate every liquidation against them explicitly.

title: ERC4626-style vault flow lacks visible anti-donation protections
rationale: Vault share math can be manipulated when early deposits or donations distort the exchange rate and the implementation has no virtual shares, offsets or minimum liquidity protections.
recommendation: Use virtual shares/assets, a decimals offset, minimum liquidity, or another documented defense against donation and first-depositor manipulation.

title: ERC4626 share conversion math lacks visible rounding controls
rationale: Vault share math is sensitive to rounding direction. Custom ERC4626 conversions that use raw multiplication and division without explicit rounding intent can leak value between depositors and redeemers.
recommendation: Use hardened mulDiv-style math with explicit rounding direction for convert/preview functions and verify behavior across low-liquidity edge cases.

title: Deposit accounting assumes full transfer amount for fee-on-transfer tokens
rationale: Protocols that credit deposits using the requested transfer amount instead of the actual received balance can be broken by fee-on-transfer or deflationary tokens.
recommendation: Measure balanceBefore/balanceAfter and account based on actual tokens received rather than trusting the transfer input amount.

title: Accounting appears to rely on raw token balances and may break for rebasing assets
rationale: Protocols that use token.balanceOf(address(this)) as primary accounting without shares or an index can behave incorrectly when assets rebase or otherwise change balances asynchronously.
recommendation: Use share-based accounting or an index/scaled-balance model instead of trusting raw token balances as the canonical user-accounting source.

title: Uniswap-style swap callback lacks visible pool verification
rationale: AMM callback functions such as uniswapV3SwapCallback must validate the caller. Without callback validation, arbitrary callers may trigger settlement logic directly.
recommendation: Validate the callback sender against the expected pool or use the protocol's callback validation helper.

title: Upgradeable contract lacks visible storage-gap reservation
rationale: Upgradeable contracts often reserve storage gaps to reduce upgrade collision risk. Missing gaps are not always exploitable on their own, but they increase maintenance and storage-layout risk over time.
recommendation: Reserve storage slots with a documented __gap pattern or apply a verified storage-layout discipline before future upgrades.

title: Token approval flow may be exposed to allowance race conditions
rationale: Directly changing a non-zero ERC20 allowance to another non-zero value can allow race-condition abuse on tokens that follow the classic approve semantics.
recommendation: Reset approvals to zero first or use forceApprove/safeIncreaseAllowance-style patterns where supported.

title: No event definitions found
rationale: Key state changes should emit events to support monitoring, analytics and incident response.
recommendation: Emit events for config changes, purchases, claims, draws, staking changes and admin actions.

title: Whitelist flow does not show nonce or deadline protection
rationale: LaunchPad whitelist flows typically need replay protection and explicit signature domain binding.
recommendation: Include signer, chainId, nonce, deadline and claimed status in whitelist verification logic.
